Post_Text: A parent volunteer drove three of your students to a field trip. She caused a wreck. Someone got hurt. The attorney isn't suing her. He's suing your school. Here's the policy you think covers this, and why it doesn't:
A parent volunteer drove three of your students to a field trip and caused a wreck. The policy you'd reach for sits this one out. (Full newsletter — see linkedin-newsletter.md)
"The driver has their own insurance." True. It pays first, then it runs out. Then the lawyer turns to the school standing right behind it. Personal limits are a first layer, not a shield:
Someone drives for your school in a car the school doesn't own. Confirm who pays before there's a wreck, not after. (Checklist + matrix — see company-page-checklist.md)
Notes: Post from company page; consider PDF lead-capture version

Post_Text: Your commercial auto policy covers vehicles the school owns. A staff member's car on a supply run is not one of them. Guess who's exposed the second that car gets in a wreck on your errand:

Hired and Non-Owned Auto. The name is terrible. The gap it fills is everywhere. Field trips, bank runs, coaches hauling athletes, volunteers driving kids. None of it feels like "the school driving." That's exactly why nobody insures it:
Some personal auto policies quietly exclude coverage when the car is used for business. A volunteer driving your students may not know that. You'll both find out at the worst possible moment:

This is one of the cheapest gaps to close in your entire insurance program. Often an inexpensive endorsement you already qualify for. Which is why it's so frustrating that it's the one most schools are missing:

One question for whoever handles your school's insurance: if a volunteer caused a serious-injury accident tomorrow driving their own car for your school, and their policy maxed out, who pays the rest? If you can't answer that, read this:
